skill.name: request-refactor-plan
skill.description: Create a detailed refactor plan with tiny commits via user interview, then file it as a GitHub issue. Use when user wants to plan a refactor, create a refactoring RFC, or break a refactor into safe incremental steps.
skill.summary: Guides Claude through a structured interview to produce a incremental refactor plan filed as a GitHub issue.
skill.author: mattpocock

security_audit.summary: The static analyzer flagged 5 'weak cryptographic algorithm' findings, but all are false positives. The skill is a pure prompt template (69 lines) with no executable code, no network calls, no filesystem access, and no external commands. The flagged lines (3, 8, 22, 56, 62) contain natural language words like 'interview', 'scope', and 'test' that the pattern matcher misidentified as crypto-related. No security risk was found after semantic review.

content.user_title: Plan Refactors with Tiny Safe Commits
content.value_statement: Refactors often fail because they are too large and poorly scoped. This skill drives a structured interview to produce a clear, incremental refactor plan filed as a GitHub issue.
actual_capabilities: Guides Claude through a step-by-step interview to gather refactor requirements
Explores the repository to verify user assertions about the current codebase
Presents alternative implementation options to the user for discussion
Breaks the refactor into the smallest possible commits that keep the codebase working
Generates a GitHub issue using a structured refactor plan template
Documents testing decisions and out-of-scope items in the issue
limitations: Does not execute the refactor itself, it only plans it
Requires a connected GitHub environment to file the issue
Depends on user availability to answer detailed interview questions
The generated plan uses plain English descriptions and excludes file paths or code snippets

use_cases.title: Senior engineer planning a large refactor
use_cases.description: An engineer wants to break a complex refactor into safe incremental steps before writing any code.

use_cases.title: Tech lead creating a team RFC
use_cases.description: A tech lead needs a standardized refactor plan document to share with the team for review and alignment.

use_cases.title: Open source maintainer scoping contributions
use_cases.description: A maintainer wants to create a detailed issue describing a desired refactor for potential contributors.

prompt_templates.title: Basic refactor request
prompt_templates.prompt: I want to refactor the authentication module to use the new session library. Use the request-refactor-plan skill to help me plan this out.
prompt_templates.scenario: Quick start when you have a clear refactor goal

prompt_templates.title: Vague refactor idea
prompt_templates.prompt: Our API layer is a mess. Help me think through a refactor plan by interviewing me about the current pain points and what we want to achieve.
prompt_templates.scenario: Starting from a rough problem description

prompt_templates.title: Refactor with test concerns
prompt_templates.prompt: I need to refactor the billing system but I am worried about test coverage. Use the request-refactor-plan skill to help me think through the scope and testing strategy.
prompt_templates.scenario: When test coverage is a concern

prompt_templates.title: Full interview mode
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the request-refactor-plan skill to interview me thoroughly about my refactor idea. I want you to be extremely detailed and challenge my assumptions before we file the GitHub issue.
prompt_templates.scenario: Comprehensive planning for a major refactor

output_examples.input: I want to refactor the database connection pooling to support multiple database types.
output: Problem Statement: The current database connection pool only supports PostgreSQL, blocking adoption of MySQL and SQLite backends.
Solution: Introduce a connection pool abstraction with adapter pattern supporting multiple database types.
Commits: Step 1 - Add a ConnectionPool interface with existing behavior. Step 2 - Implement PostgreSQL adapter. Step 3 - Add MySQL adapter behind a feature flag. Step 4 - Add tests for each adapter.
Decision Document: Pool size, timeout values, and adapter interface boundaries were decided during interview.
Testing Decisions: Each adapter must have integration tests against a real database instance.

output_examples.input: Our error handling is inconsistent across the codebase.
output: Problem Statement: Errors are handled inconsistently, with some modules throwing and others returning result objects.
Solution: Standardize on a single error handling approach with a typed Result wrapper.
Out of Scope: Changing the actual error messages or adding new error types beyond the standard ones.

best_practices: Be as detailed as possible when answering the interview questions to produce a stronger plan
Review the generated GitHub issue before filing to ensure it captures your intent
Use this skill early in the refactor process, before writing any code
anti_patterns: Do not use this skill when you already have a clear, small refactor that needs no planning
Do not include specific file paths or code snippets in the plan, as they become outdated quickly
Do not skip the testing decisions section, as it is critical for safe refactoring

faq.question: What does this skill do?
faq.answer: It guides Claude through a structured interview to produce a detailed refactor plan and file it as a GitHub issue.

faq.question: Does it execute the refactor?
faq.answer: No, it only plans the refactor. You or your team will execute the commits afterward.

faq.question: How detailed should my answers be?
faq.answer: Extremely detailed. The quality of the plan depends directly on the detail of your interview responses.

faq.question: Will it include code in the GitHub issue?
faq.answer: No, the plan deliberately excludes file paths and code snippets because they become outdated quickly.

faq.question: Can I skip interview steps?
faq.answer: Yes, the skill states that you may skip steps you do not consider necessary.

faq.question: Does it work with any repository?
faq.answer: Yes, the skill explores whatever repository you are working in to verify your assertions.
